sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 20, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: White chamber at rows 32-36, cols 16-19 contains blue projectile. Player at rows 37-39 below chamber. Target B frame at rows 28-31 above chamber. SPACE previously didn't work from this position.
- Plan: Try SPACE again and carefully check if projectile moves or if chamber changes. Also check if player position matters.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 19 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The white block changed! Before SPACE, it was at rows 32-36 with 16 pixels. After SPACE, it's at rows 36-36 with only 4 pixels. So SPACE caused the white block to shrink from 5 rows to 1 row!
This means SPACE is "consuming" the white block, row by row. Each SPACE press removes the top row of the white block.
Let me check the ascii to see the current state of the chamber area.
The white block was:
- Row 32: WWWW
- Row 33: WbbW
- Row 34: WbbW
- Row 35: WWWW
- Row 36: WWWW
After SPACE, only row 36 remains with 4 pixels (WWWW). So the top 4 rows were removed.
But wait, the blue projectile is still at rows 33-34. How can that be if rows 33-34 were removed?
Let me check the ascii again to see the current state.
Actually, maybe the white block moved down. Let me check the ascii for rows 30-40.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: SPACE shrinks the white chamber! Before: rows 32-36 (16 pixels). After: rows 36-36 (4 pixels). Each SPACE press removes rows from the chamber. The blue projectile remains at rows 33-34.
Plan: Check ascii to see current chamber state, then continue pressing SPACE to remove the chamber completely, allowing upward movement to target B.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current ascii around the chamber
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Area around target B and chamber:")
for r in range(28, 42):
    if r < len(ascii_lines):
        row_str = ascii_lines[r]
        cols_slice = row_str[14:22] if len(row_str) > 21 else row_str
        print(f"Row {r}: '{cols_slice}'")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see! The chamber changed color and position:
- Rows 32-35: Now dark gray (G) instead of white (W)
- Row 36: Still white (W)
- Rows 33-34: Still have blue projectile (bb)
So the white block didn't shrink - it changed! The top part (rows 32-35) turned from white (W) to dark gray (G), and only row 36 remains white.
This is interesting. The dark gray (G) is different from the target frame's dark gray (c). Let me check the segmentation again.
Wait, looking at the ascii:
- Row 28: `wwccccww` - target B frame (c = charcoal)
- Row 32: `wwGGGGww` - G = dark gray
- Row 36: `wwWWWWww` - W = white
So the chamber has:
- Rows 32-35: dark gray (G) walls with blue projectile inside
- Row 36: white (W) bottom
This is different from before. Before SPACE, the entire chamber was white (W). After SPACE, the top 4 rows became dark gray (G).
So SPACE doesn't remove the chamber - it changes the chamber's color from white to dark gray. This might be a "charging" or "activating" mechanism.
Let me think about what this means. Maybe:
1. The chamber starts as white (inactive).
2. SPACE activates the chamber, turning it dark gray.
3. When the chamber is fully activated (all dark gray), the projectile can be launched.
But the bottom row (row 36) is still white. So I need more SPACE presses to activate the entire chamber.
Wait, but the chamber is 5 rows tall (rows 32-36). After one SPACE, rows 32-35 became dark gray, leaving only row 36 white. So maybe one more SPACE will activate the entire chamber.
Let me try SPACE again and see if row 36 also becomes dark gray.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The white block had 16 pixels before SPACE, and now it has 4 pixels (only row 36). The dark gray part (rows 32-35) has how many pixels?
Looking at the ascii:
- Row 32: GGGG (4 G's)
- Row 33: GbbG (2 G's)
- Row 34: GbbG (2 G's)
- Row 35: GGGG (4 G's)
Total G's: 4 + 2 + 2 + 4 = 12. So the dark gray part has 12 pixels.
Let me check the segmentation to see if there's a dark gray object with 12 pixels.
Actually, I already know the target frames have 12 pixels each (dark gray, color 'c'). But the chamber walls are now 'G' (dark gray), not 'c' (charcoal).
Let me check the segmentation for 'G' objects.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: SPACE changed chamber color from white (W) to dark gray (G) for rows 32-35. Row 36 remains white. Chamber is "activating" - each SPACE press converts white rows to dark gray.
Plan: Press SPACE again to activate the remaining white row, then check if the projectile can be launched.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check segmentation for G objects
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("All objects:")
for n in nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Try SPACE again
print("\nTrying SPACE again...")
predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE activates remaining white row')
action(['SPACE'])

# Check ascii again
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nAfter second SPACE:")
for r in range(28, 42):
    if r < len(ascii_lines):
        row_str = ascii_lines[r]
        cols_slice = row_str[14:22] if len(row_str) > 21 else row_str
        print(f"Row {r}: '{cols_slice}'")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
